literal: action, deed, rite; from √kṛ 'to do, to make'
invariant_definition: *Karma* is much broader than the English word 'action.' Its background lies in the grammar of agency: Pāṇinian *kāraka* theory analyzes a process through roles such as *kartṛ*, *karaṇa*, and *karman*, so *karma* already belongs to a structured account of who acts, by what means, and upon what. Philosophical schools then extend that grammar into causal theory. Mīmāṃsā treats *karma* as Veda-enjoined performance under *codanā* and *bhāvanā*, with *apūrva* explaining delayed ritual efficacy. Yoga and Vedānta foreground the sequence of action, residual trace, and fruition - *karma*, *saṃskāra*, and *vipāka* - while Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika often speaks of *adṛṣṭa* as the unseen carryover connecting deed and result. Thus *karma* can name the act, the ritual performance, the residual deposit left by action, or the whole economy by which embodied life is steered. Buddhists reconfigure it around intention and conditioned continuity without a permanent self; Jains construe it as karmic matter binding the *jīva*. Vedānta inherits that whole apparatus and classifies karmic residue as *sañcita*, *prārabdha*, and *āgāmi* while disputing whether *jñāna*, *bhakti*, or disciplined action is decisive for release.

school_framing.shared_core: All schools that retain the *śabda* *karma* mean **action together with the residual efficacy by which it links agent, cause, and later result**. The grammar is Pāṇinian (the *kāraka* role *karman* fixes 'that which is acted upon'); the metaphysical extension is the action-trace-fruition triad. Schools then differ on (i) the carrier of residual efficacy (*apūrva*, *adṛṣṭa*, *vāsanā*, *karma-pudgala*, *kārma-mala*), (ii) whether agency belongs to *puruṣa* or to *prakṛti*/*citta*/Śiva-*śakti*, and (iii) whether *karma* itself or *jñāna*/*bhakti*/*śūnyatā* removes bondage.
school_framing.register_axes_note: Mīmāṃsā speaks in RN+SL; Advaita and Viśiṣṭādvaita in S+E; Sāṅkhya in C+S; Buddhism in Φ+S without an enduring agent; Jainism gives *karma* a partially *different concept* (literal material *pudgala* adhering to the *jīva*). Where schools share the same register and scope (e.g., whether *karma* can directly produce *mokṣa*), the disagreement is real (Mīmāṃsā vs. Advaita on *jñāna-karma-samuccaya*; AF1).

per_school.definition: *Karma* belongs to the sphere of *avidyā*, *kāma*, and *kartṛtva*; because its effect is produced and therefore *anitya*, *mokṣa* cannot be a *karma-kārya* and depends on *jñāna* alone [[UpS I.11.15](cite://sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.11.15)] [[UpS I.17.7-9](cite://sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.17.7-9)]. The school therefore rejects *jñāna-karma-samuccaya*: *mahāvākya*-born cognition removes beginningless *mithyājñāna* directly, so no co-functioning ritual means remains to be supplied [[NS 1.98-99](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/1.98-99)] [[PV p. 164](cite://prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/brahma-vicāre kim-ānantaryam atha-śabdāvagamyam)]. Within *vyavahāra*, however, agenthood with respect to *sukṛta* and *duṣkṛta karma* attaches to the *buddhi*-situated *ātma-citi-pratibimba*, not to the *nirvikriya* self [[SS I.327](cite://sarvajnatman/samksepa-sariraka/1.327)].

For the aspirant who has discerned that *kevala-karma* yields only an *alpa-sthira-phala*, *brahma-jijñāsā* arises as the pursuit of the infinite and abiding end, so ritual action is not denied but resituated within a higher *puruṣārtha*-order [[ŚrīBh 3](cite://ramanuja/shri-bhasya/3)]. The moral weight of karma remains fully real, for the *jīva* stands before Mukunda at the moment of *vipāka* bearing the burden of its own *nindita-karma* and seeking refuge by *prapatti* [[YStr 22-24](cite://yamuna/stotra-ratna/22-24)]. Rāmānuja's grammatical insistence that *brahma-jijñāsā* takes Brahman as its *karman* marks liberating inquiry as ordered to the Lord rather than exhausted by ritual performance as such [[ŚrīBh 5](cite://ramanuja/shri-bhasya/5)] [[ŚrīBh 11](cite://ramanuja/shri-bhasya/11)].

*Karma* is a real constituent of the ordered world, yet its very being is *paratantra*: action, like substance, time, and the *jīva*, stands only through Hari's *anugraha* [[Anuvy. 1.1.13-15](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/1.1.13-15)]. It is therefore to be undertaken as *bhagavad-bhakti-jñāna-vairāgya-pūrvaka* and as *sva-karmaṇā tam abhyarcya*, not as an autonomous generator of fruit [[KN p. 257a](cite://madhva/karma-nirnaya/p. 257a)] [[KN p. 257c](cite://madhva/karma-nirnaya/p. 257c)]. Action devoid of dedication to Īśvara remains *abhadra*, whereas faithful conformity to Bhagavat-teaching becomes the very mode through which one is released from karmic bondage [[KN p. 257b](cite://madhva/karma-nirnaya/p. 257b)] [[KN p. 257e](cite://madhva/karma-nirnaya/p. 257e)].

*Karma* appears here as the binding *granthi* of embodied consciousness, a knot severed not by bare ritualism but by mantra-bhajana suffused with *bhakti*, *japa*, *homa*, *yajana*, and *dhyāna* [[KD 8.96](cite://kesava-kasmiri/krama-dipika/8.96)]. Its overcoming is therefore articulated as entry into Murāri's *nitya-śuddha-pada*, where the practitioner stands awakened through Bhagavat-oriented practice rather than through self-standing action [[KD 8.96](cite://kesava-kasmiri/krama-dipika/8.96)].

*Karma* in Sāṅkhya is absorbed into the economy of the *liṅga* and its *bhāvas*: the subtle body transmigrates as permeated by dispositions, while *dharma* and cognate states are counted among the operative *bhāvas* rooted in the internal apparatus [[SK 40](cite://isvarakrsna/sankhya-karika/40)] [[SK 43](cite://isvarakrsna/sankhya-karika/43)]. The vertical ordering of rebirth is then specified through *dharma* and *adharma*: the former carries one upward, the latter downward, whereas bondage persists through *viparyaya* and ceases only through *jñāna* [[SK 44](cite://isvarakrsna/sankhya-karika/44)]. Accordingly, karmic efficacy belongs to prakṛti's saṃsāric machinery rather than to *puruṣa*, whose liberation is the result of discriminative knowledge rather than of action as such [[SK 40](cite://isvarakrsna/sankhya-karika/40)] [[SK 44](cite://isvarakrsna/sankhya-karika/44)].

In Yoga, karma belongs to the nexus *kleśa-karma-vipāka-āśaya*; bound existence is structured not only by acts but by their maturation and the latent deposits they leave in the stream of experience [[YV 1.24](cite://vijnanabhiksu/yoga-varttika/1.24)]. Īśvara is marked precisely as the *puruṣa-viśeṣa* untouched by this entire chain, so yogic discipline aims at disentanglement from karmic ripening rather than mere management of merit and demerit [[YV 1.24](cite://vijnanabhiksu/yoga-varttika/1.24)]. The turn to *īśvara-praṇidhāna* is accordingly construed as a special *samādhi-bhāvanā* leading beyond the ordinary karmic circuitry of the afflicted mind [[YV 1.23](cite://vijnanabhiksu/yoga-varttika/1.23)].

*Karma* in Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika has a double technical life: it is, first, the *padārtha* of motion, the cause of conjunction and disjunction inhering in a single substance, and, second, the morally consequential activity whose continuation keeps the chain of birth and suffering in place [[VS 1.1.16](cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/1.1.16)] [[NyS 1.1.2](cite://gautama/nyaya-sutra/1.1.2)]. Desire and aversion generate *dharma* and *adharma*; from these follow renewed conjunction and disjunction, while release is described as the non-arising of such connections [[VS 6.2.17-19](cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/6.2.17-19)] [[VS 5.2.20](cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/5.2.20)]. Later Vaiśeṣika synopsis makes the moral sense fully explicit by defining *dharma* and *adharma* as effects of enjoined and prohibited action, so karmic causality remains a real determinant of the self's history [[TSaṅg 71-72](cite://annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/71-72)].

For Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā, *karma* is paradigmatically Veda-enjoined *kriyā*: Jaimini defines *dharma* through *codanā* and then states that *karma*-terms have *bhāva* as their meaning, from which the act itself is understood [[MS 1.1.2](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.1.2)] [[MS 2.1.1](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/2.1.1)]. Because the Veda is *kriyārtha*, ritual action cannot be reduced to a merely descriptive occurrence, and its fruit depends on the specifically injunction-grounded novelty called *apūrva* [[MS 1.2.1](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.2.1)] [[MS 1.2.19](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.2.19)]. Śabara makes the temporal structure explicit: sacrifice produces *apūrva*, and from that *apūrva* the fruit arises later, which is why correctly configured performance remains the primary bearer of *dharma* and *phala* [[ŚBh on MS 7.4.1](cite://shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/7.4.1-apurva)].

*Karma* and *kleśa* are not self-grounding realities; they arise from *vikalpa* and the wider machinery of *prapañca* [[MMK 18.5](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.5)]. *Mokṣa* is named as the exhaustion of karma and affliction, but that exhaustion occurs only where proliferative construction is brought to rest in *śūnyatā* [[MMK 18.5](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.5)].

Karmic continuity is carried not by an enduring agent but by *vāsanā*-bearing transformation within consciousness itself; when one maturation is exhausted, karmic perfumings generate a further maturation together with the habitual duality of grasper and grasped [[Triṃ 19](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/19)]. The school accordingly rejects the displacement of karmic seed and fruit into an external domain, arguing that the *karma-vāsanā* remains lodged in the *vijñāna-saṃtāna* and ripens there as a further transformation of consciousness [[Vvs 6-8](cite://vasubandhu/vimsatika/Karma, Vāsanā, and Scriptural Objection)].

In Pratyabhijñā-Trika, the bound subject is *karmabandhana* because its own *svātantrya* has contracted under māyā and no longer shines as unrestricted lordship [[ĪPK 3.2.2](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/3.2.2)]. The school's specific category is *kārma-mala*: when agency falls into *abodha*, karma becomes the limited producer of *janma* and *bhoga* within differentiated experience [[ĪPK 3.2.5](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/3.2.5)] [[ĪPV 3.2.5](cite://abhinavagupta/isvara-pratyabhijna-vimarshini/3.2.5)]. Kṣemarāja accordingly glosses *kārma-mala* as the contraction of *kriyā-śakti* into *śubhāśubhānuṣṭhāna*, so karma is a diminished form of Śiva's own *kartṛtva*, not an ultimate principle independent of consciousness [[PH 9](cite://ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/9)].

In Jainism, karma is karmically efficacious *pudgala* entering the *jīva* through the activities of body, speech, and mind; these yogas are the very form of *āsrava* [[TS 6.1-2](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/6.1-2)]. Bondage arises because the passion-laden soul appropriates pudgalas fit for karma, and that bondage is analyzed through *prakṛti*, *sthiti*, *anubhava*, and *pradeśa* [[TS 8.1-3](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/8.1-3)]. Liberation is the total release from karma, reached through *saṃvara* and *nirjarā* and completed as the exhaustive dissociation from karmic matter [[TS 9.1-3](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/9.1-3)] [[TS 10.2](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/10.2)].
per_school.register_tag: M+S; reflective reader (karma as material pudgala)

translator_note: Avoid the lazy glosses 'work,' 'fate,' and 'what goes around comes around.' 'Work' strips away the causal and ritual apparatus; 'fate' turns *karma* into passive destiny; the popular proverb makes it sound like a vague moral boomerang. *Karma* names an action-structure and its residual efficacy inside a theory of agency, causation, and rebirth.
